source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A parallel-plate capacitor is made of circular plates as shown in Fig. 2.10. The voltage across the plates (supplied by long resistanceless lead wires) has the time dependence $V=V_{0} \cos \omega t$. Assume $d \ll a \ll c / \omega$, so that fringing of the electric field and retardation may be ignored. What is the magnetic field in region II? Relate the discontinuity of $B$ accross a plate to the surface current in the plate.

Geometry/spatial setup of circular capacitor plates and region labeling are central to interpreting the target field expression.
multi_step: The task asks for two linked outputs: the magnetic field in region II and the relation between the field discontinuity and surface current.
Solving requires combining capacitor geometry with time-varying voltage and electromagnetic laws, not just reading off a single fact.
The answer contains a derived formula plus a boundary-condition relation, suggesting at least two reasoning steps.
verifiability: The provided answer is a compact symbolic result with explicit formula structure, which is amenable to rule-based or symbolic checking.
`answer_type` is effectively formula/set style, and `answer_present=true` with short, well-defined target expressions.
`registry_passed=true` and the image asset exists, so the sample is sufficiently registered for downstream checking.

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A parallel-plate capacitor is made of circular plates as shown in Fig. 2.10. The voltage across the plates (supplied by long resistanceless lead wires) has the time dependence $V=V_{0} \cos \omega t$. Assume $d \ll a \ll c / \omega$, so that fringing of the electric field and retardation may be ignored. What is the magnetic field in region II? candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: images 字段提供的是 PIL 图像对象占位字符串，不是实际文件路径或 URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: In Ampere's circuital law $$\oint_{L}\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=\mu_{0}I,$$ the direction of flow of $I$ and the sense of traversing $L$ follow the right-handed screw rule. At time $t$, $I$ flows along the $-z$-direction and by axial symmetry $$\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}=B_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}\,e_{\phi}.$$ Hence $$B_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}=-\frac{\mu_{0}I}{2\pi r}=\frac{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}a^{2}V_{0}\omega}{2dr}\sin\omega t.$$ Thus $$\mathbf{B}_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}-\mathbf{B}_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{I})}=\frac{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\left(a^{2}-r^{2}\right)V_{0}\omega}{2dr}\sin\omega t\,\mathbf{e}_{r}=\mu_{0}j_{l},$$ or $$\mathbf{n}\times\left(\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}-\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{I})}\right)=\mu_{0}\mathbf{j}.$$ This is just the boundary condition for the magnetic field intensity $$H_{t}^{(\mathrm{II})}-H_{t}^{(\mathrm{I})}=j_{l}.$$

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: In Ampere's circuital law $$\oint_{L}\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=\mu_{0}I,$$ the direction of flow of $I$ and the sense of traversing $L$ follow the right-handed screw rule. At time $t$, $I$ flows along the $-z$-direction and by axial symmetry $$\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}=B_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}\,e_{\phi}.$$ Hence $$B_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}=-\frac{\mu_{0}I}{2\pi r}=\frac{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}a^{2}V_{0}\omega}{2dr}\sin\omega t.$$ Thus $$\mathbf{B}_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{II})}-\mathbf{B}_{\phi}^{(\mathrm{I})}=\frac{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\left(a^{2}-r^{2}\right)V_{0}\omega}{2dr}\sin\omega t\,\mathbf{e}_{r}=\mu_{0}j_{l},$$ or $$\mathbf{n}\times\left(\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{II})}-\mathbf{B}^{(\mathrm{I})}\right)=\mu_{0}\mathbf{j}.$$ This is just the boundary condition for the magnetic field intensity $$H_{t}^{(\mathrm{II})}-H_{t}^{(\mathrm{I})}=j_{l}.$$
